policy_statement: Implement mandatory certification for sustainable forest management.
mechanism_description: It implements mandatory certification for sustainable forest management to improve forest practices and preserve ecosystem integrity and forest carbon stocks.
primary_causal_pathway: Improved land management (forests) → ↓ deforestation/degradation + maintained biomass/soil carbon → ↓ net CO₂ emissions + sustained carbon sink
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Forest owners/operators must meet sustainable management standards (e.g., reduced-impact logging, regeneration set, riparian buffers, protection of intact/high-carbon areas, monitoring and compliance) to obtain/keep certification.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced forest disturbance lowers biomass loss and soil carbon release; improved regeneration and protection maintain or enhance long-term carbon sequestration and ecosystem function.  
(3) Outcome: Lower net greenhouse gas emissions (primarily CO₂) via avoided degradation/deforestation and preserved forest carbon stocks, with co-benefits for biodiversity and ecosystem resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding CO₂ emissions (and sustaining carbon sinks) by preventing forest degradation/deforestation through improved forest management.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Policy drives improved forest management → preserves standing forest carbon and reduces degradation/deforestation → reduces net CO₂ emissions / sustains sink capacity.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although certification can improve resource efficiency in timber supply chains or governance, the main climate effect is biophysical—maintaining ecosystems and their carbon stocks—matching Nature-Based Solutions more directly than Mitigation/Resource Efficiency framing.  
3) Edge cases applied: If certification primarily targets timber efficiency or product substitution benefits, those could be secondary; however, across policies using this mechanism, the consistent intended effect is ecosystem/carbon preservation via better forest stewardship.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any “sustainable forest management certification” mechanism operates by changing on-the-ground forest practices to maintain ecosystem integrity and carbon stocks, so the primary category remains Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., avoided deforestation vs. improved forest management vs. governance/procurement) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some certification schemes are implemented mainly through market access/procurement and may have variable additionality or leakage; in such cases, realized mitigation may be smaller, but the dominant intended pathway still runs through forest ecosystem carbon preservation.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Certification” can be voluntary in some contexts, but the text explicitly states it is “mandatory,” supporting classification as a regulatory mandate.
